stem: A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sharp central chest pain that has been present for 18 hours. The pain is worse on inspiration and when lying flat, and improves when he sits forward. He had a coryzal illness the previous week. He has no significant past medical history. His troponin T is 8 ng/L (reference less than 14 ng/L) and his ECG shows widespread concave ST elevation with PR depression in leads II, aVF, and V4 to V6.

data_table.Vitals: Temperature: 37.6 C; Heart rate: 88 bpm; Blood pressure: 124/76 mmHg; Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min; SpO2: 99% on room air

lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

options.text: Aspirin 300 mg loading dose followed by dual antiplatelet therapy for 12 months
options.rationale: Dual antiplatelet therapy is indicated for acute coronary syndromes, not pericarditis. This patient has classic features of acute pericarditis including pleuritic positional pain, a preceding viral illness, and the characteristic ECG pattern of widespread concave ST elevation with PR depression. His troponin is normal, making ACS unlikely. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Prednisolone 50 mg orally daily tapered over 4 weeks as initial anti-inflammatory therapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are associated with increased recurrence of pericarditis and should not be used as first-line therapy. They are reserved for cases refractory to NSAIDs and colchicine, or when there is a specific indication such as autoimmune pericarditis. When used, low-dose prednisolone (0.25 to 0.5 mg/kg/day) is preferred with slow taper. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Paracetamol 1 g four times daily for 1 to 2 weeks with activity restriction until review
options.rationale: Paracetamol alone is insufficient for the treatment of acute pericarditis. The anti-inflammatory properties of NSAIDs (or aspirin) are essential for treating the underlying pericardial inflammation. Colchicine must be added to reduce the risk of recurrence, which occurs in approximately 30% of cases without it. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily for 1 to 2 weeks and colchicine 500 mcg twice daily for 3 months
options.rationale: First-line treatment for acute pericarditis is a high-dose NSAID (ibuprofen 600 mg TDS for 1 to 2 weeks, then taper) combined with colchicine 500 mcg BD for 3 months. The COPE and ICAP trials demonstrated that adding colchicine to NSAID therapy significantly reduces pericarditis recurrence. Gastroprotection should be considered with high-dose NSAID use. Activity restriction is advised until symptoms resolve and inflammatory markers normalise. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Ibuprofen 200 mg three times daily for 5 days with no additional anti-inflammatory therapy
options.rationale: This dose of ibuprofen is subtherapeutic for acute pericarditis, where 600 mg TDS is recommended. Additionally, omitting colchicine is a significant omission as the COPE trial demonstrated a halving of recurrence rates with the addition of colchicine. A 5-day treatment course is also too short. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: This young man presents with acute pericarditis following a viral illness. The diagnosis is supported by pleuritic chest pain that is positional (worse lying flat, better sitting forward), widespread concave ST elevation with PR depression on ECG, and a normal troponin. Treatment is ibuprofen 600 mg TDS for 1 to 2 weeks with gradual taper, combined with colchicine 500 mcg BD for 3 months to reduce the approximately 30% recurrence rate. Corticosteroids increase recurrence risk and should be avoided first-line. Patients should restrict exercise until symptom resolution and normalisation of CRP.
key_points: Acute pericarditis: pleuritic positional chest pain, widespread concave ST elevation, PR depression on ECG
First-line: ibuprofen 600 mg TDS for 1 to 2 weeks plus colchicine 500 mcg BD for 3 months
Corticosteroids increase pericarditis recurrence and are not first-line therapy
explanation.pearls: Colchicine 500 mcg BD for 3 months halves the recurrence rate of acute pericarditis (COPE trial). Patients weighing less than 70 kg should receive colchicine 500 mcg once daily. Exercise restriction is recommended until symptoms resolve and CRP normalises, typically a minimum of 3 months for athletes.

_archived_reason: duplicate of med-pericard-001 (pericarditis) / obgyn-deficit2-001 (ectopic) per dedupe audit 2026-06-01
